 {"id":"wa-37x","title":"[EPIC] Reliability Hardening: Circuit Breakers, Chaos Testing, Graceful Degradation","description":"# [EPIC] Reliability Hardening

## Mission
Make wa **resilient to failures** at every level—external services, internal components, and edge cases.

## Why This Matters
wa controls terminal interactions. Failures must be handled gracefully:
- Browser automation fails → do not spam retries
- WezTerm CLI hangs → do not block forever
- DB corrupts → continue observing, degrade gracefully

Reliability is a trust multiplier. Users need confidence that wa will not make things worse when something goes wrong.

## Components

### 1. Circuit Breaker Pattern
Stop hammering failed services:
```
Service: browser_auth
Status: OPEN (tripped 3 failures)
Last failure: 2026-01-18T14:32:01 (timeout)
Retry after: 2026-01-18T14:37:01 (5 min cooldown)
```

Circuit breakers for:
- Browser automation (auth flows)
- `caut` CLI wrapper
- WezTerm CLI (if consistently failing)
- External webhooks

### 2. Watchdog/Heartbeat System
Monitor wa's own health:
- Heartbeat thread checks main loop liveness
- Deadlock detection (no progress for N seconds)
- Auto-restart on unrecoverable hang
- Crash report with diagnostic context

### 3. Chaos Testing Harness
Inject failures to prove resilience:
- Kill WezTerm mid-capture
- Corrupt DB file
- Simulate network timeouts
- Exhaust disk space
- Fill queues to capacity

Chaos tests validate:
- Data integrity after failures
- Graceful degradation behavior
- Recovery procedures work

### 4. Retry with Exponential Backoff
Standardized retry policy:
- Initial: 100ms
- Max: 30s
- Backoff factor: 2x
- Jitter: ±10%
- Max attempts: configurable

Applied to:
- WezTerm CLI calls
- DB writes (transient lock conflicts)
- External service calls

### 5. Graceful Degradation Modes
When components fail, continue with reduced functionality:
- DB read-only → continue observing, queue writes
- Pattern engine error → skip detection, keep ingesting
- Workflow failure → pause workflow, keep observing
- WezTerm CLI unavailable → retry with backoff, surface status

## Observability
All reliability mechanisms emit structured logs:
- Circuit state changes
- Retry attempts and outcomes
- Degradation mode transitions
- Watchdog warnings

## Testing
- Unit tests: Circuit breaker state machine
- Integration tests: Retry behavior under failure injection
- Chaos tests: System survives failure scenarios
- Load tests: Behavior under resource exhaustion

## Success Criteria
- Circuit breakers prevent retry storms
- Watchdog detects and recovers from hangs
- Chaos tests pass for critical failure scenarios
- Graceful degradation maintains core observability

## Acceptance Criteria
- Circuit breakers, retries, and watchdogs prevent runaway failures.
- Graceful degradation keeps core commands usable.
- Chaos harness runs without crashes and produces actionable logs.

 {"id":"wa-37x.1","title":"Circuit breaker infrastructure: state machine, cooldowns, and status reporting","description":"
# Circuit Breaker Infrastructure

## Purpose
Implement a generic circuit breaker pattern to prevent retry storms when services fail.

## State Machine
```
     failures < threshold
CLOSED ◄────────────────────────► OPEN
  │                                  │
  │ failure >= threshold             │ cooldown elapsed
  └──────────────────►               ▼
                              HALF-OPEN
                                  │
                    success → CLOSED
                    failure → OPEN
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct CircuitBreaker {
    name: String,
    state: CircuitState,
    failure_count: u32,
    failure_threshold: u32,
    cooldown: Duration,
    last_failure: Option<Instant>,
}

impl CircuitBreaker {
    pub fn call<T>(&self, f: impl FnOnce() -> Result<T>) -> Result<T, CircuitError>;
    pub fn record_success(&mut self);
    pub fn record_failure(&mut self, error: &Error);
    pub fn status(&self) -> CircuitStatus;
}
```

## Circuit Breakers to Implement
- browser_auth: For Playwright auth flows
- caut_cli: For account usage queries
- wezterm_cli: For pane operations
- webhook: For notification delivery

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa doctor --circuits

Circuit Breaker Status:
  browser_auth: CLOSED (healthy)
  caut_cli:     OPEN (3 failures, retry in 4m32s)
  wezterm_cli:  CLOSED (healthy)
  webhook:      HALF-OPEN (testing...)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CircuitBreaker struct with state machine
- [ ] Configurable thresholds and cooldowns
- [ ] Status visible in wa doctor
- [ ] Structured logging on state changes

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-37x.2","title":"Watchdog/heartbeat system: deadlock detection and auto-recovery","description":"
# Watchdog/Heartbeat System

## Purpose
Monitor wa's own health and recover from hangs or deadlocks.

## How It Works
1. Main loop updates heartbeat timestamp each iteration
2. Watchdog thread checks heartbeat every N seconds
3. If heartbeat stale > threshold, trigger recovery

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct Watchdog {
    heartbeat: Arc<AtomicU64>,  // Unix timestamp
    stale_threshold: Duration,
    recovery_action: RecoveryAction,
}

pub enum RecoveryAction {
    LogWarning,
    DumpDiagnostics,
    ForceRestart,
}
```

## Recovery Sequence
1. Log warning with stack traces
2. Dump diagnostic state (queue depths, lock holders)
3. If still stale after N seconds: force restart
4. Generate crash report with diagnostics

## Diagnostic Dump
When watchdog triggers:
- Thread states
- Queue depths
- Lock holder info (if available)
- Recent event log
- Memory usage

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa status --health

Watchdog:
  Main loop: healthy (last heartbeat 0.1s ago)
  DB writer: healthy (queue: 3 items)
  Ingest: healthy (lag: 15ms)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Heartbeat updated each main loop iteration
- [ ] Watchdog thread monitors heartbeat
- [ ] Recovery actions trigger at configurable threshold
- [ ] Diagnostic dump provides useful context
- [ ] Crash report generated on force restart

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

# Chaos Testing Harness

## Purpose
Inject failures systematically to validate wa's resilience under adverse conditions.

## Fault Injection Points
```rust
pub trait FaultInjector {
    fn maybe_fail(&self, point: FaultPoint) -> Result<()>;
}

pub enum FaultPoint {
    WezTermCliCall,
    DbWrite,
    DbRead,
    BrowserAction,
    NetworkRequest,
    FileSystemOp,
}
```

## Test Scenarios

### 1. WezTerm Unavailable
- Kill WezTerm during capture
- Verify: wa degrades gracefully, resumes when available

### 2. DB Corruption
- Corrupt SQLite file mid-write
- Verify: wa detects corruption, reports error, doesn't crash

### 3. Network Timeouts
- Inject timeout on webhook calls
- Verify: circuit breaker trips, notifications queue

### 4. Resource Exhaustion
- Fill disk to capacity
- Verify: wa stops writing, logs error, continues observing

### 5. Queue Overflow
- Fill event queue beyond capacity
- Verify: backpressure applied, no data loss

## Harness Design
```rust
pub struct ChaosHarness {
    injectors: HashMap<FaultPoint, Box<dyn FaultInjector>>,
    scenario: ChaosScenario,
}

pub struct ChaosScenario {
    faults: Vec<ScheduledFault>,
    duration: Duration,
    assertions: Vec<ChaosAssertion>,
}
```

## CI Integration
- Chaos tests run in isolated environment
- Failures generate detailed reports
- Key scenarios gated on merge

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fault injection framework implemented
- [ ] At least 5 chaos scenarios defined
- [ ] All scenarios pass (system recovers)
- [ ] Chaos tests run in CI (nightly)

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-37x.4","title":"Retry with exponential backoff: standardized retry policy across all I/O","description":"
# Retry with Exponential Backoff

## Purpose
Provide a consistent, well-tuned retry mechanism for all fallible operations.

## Retry Policy
```rust
pub struct RetryPolicy {
    pub initial_delay: Duration,    // 100ms default
    pub max_delay: Duration,        // 30s default
    pub backoff_factor: f64,        // 2.0 default
    pub jitter_percent: f64,        // 10% default
    pub max_attempts: Option<u32>,  // None = infinite
}

pub async fn with_retry<T>(
    policy: &RetryPolicy,
    operation: impl AsyncFn() -> Result<T>,
) -> Result<T>;
```

## Jitter Formula
```
delay = base_delay * jitter_factor
jitter_factor = 1.0 + random(-jitter_percent, +jitter_percent)
```

## Application Points
- WezTerm CLI calls: 3 attempts, 100ms initial
- DB writes: 5 attempts, 50ms initial
- Webhook delivery: 5 attempts, 1s initial
- Browser actions: 2 attempts, 500ms initial

## Logging
Each retry logged with:
- Attempt number
- Delay applied
- Error that triggered retry

## Integration with Circuit Breaker
- Retries happen within circuit breaker
- Exceeded retries count as circuit failure
- Circuit OPEN prevents retry attempts

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] RetryPolicy struct with configurable parameters
- [ ] with_retry helper function
- [ ] Jitter implemented correctly
- [ ] All I/O points use retry policy
- [ ] Logging shows retry attempts

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-37x.5","title":"Graceful degradation modes: continue operating with reduced functionality","description":"
# Graceful Degradation Modes

## Purpose
When components fail, continue operating with reduced functionality rather than crashing.

## Degradation Scenarios

### 1. DB Read-Only Mode
**Trigger**: DB write failures (corruption, disk full)
**Behavior**:
- Continue observing panes
- Queue writes in memory (bounded)
- Log warning about degraded mode
- Resume normal operation when DB recovers

### 2. Pattern Engine Failure
**Trigger**: Pattern compilation error, regex timeout
**Behavior**:
- Skip pattern detection
- Continue ingesting output
- Log which patterns are disabled
- Partial detection if some patterns work

### 3. Workflow Engine Pause
**Trigger**: Workflow step fails repeatedly
**Behavior**:
- Pause the failing workflow
- Continue other workflows
- Keep observing for new events
- Notify user of paused workflow

### 4. WezTerm Unavailable
**Trigger**: CLI consistently fails
**Behavior**:
- Stop action attempts
- Continue any cached observation
- Poll for WezTerm availability
- Resume automatically when available

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa status

System Status: DEGRADED

Active Degradations:
  ⚠ DB writes queued (disk full) - 47 pending
  ⚠ Workflow paused: handle_usage_limits (step 3 failed)

Core Functions:
  ✓ Observation: active
  ✓ Detection: active
  ✗ Actions: degraded
```

## Recovery
- Automatic: Poll for recovery conditions
- Manual: `wa recover <component>` to retry

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each degradation mode implemented
- [ ] Status reports degraded state clearly
- [ ] Automatic recovery when conditions clear
- [ ] Manual recovery command works
- [ ] Core observation continues in all modes

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-3g9","title":"[EPIC] Performance Optimizations: Lazy Loading, Caching, Pooling","description":"# [EPIC] Performance Optimizations

## Mission
Make wa **fast and efficient** without sacrificing safety or correctness.

## Why This Matters
wa runs continuously in the background. Performance impacts:
- CPU usage (battery life on laptops)
- Memory footprint
- Latency of operations
- Scalability to many panes

Optimizations must be:
- Measurable (benchmarks prove value)
- Safe (no correctness regressions)
- Conditional (only apply when beneficial)

## Components

### 1. Lazy Pattern Compilation
Do not compile regex/Aho-Corasick engines until first use:
```rust
static PATTERN_ENGINE: Lazy<PatternEngine> = Lazy::new(|| {
    PatternEngine::compile(load_packs())
});
```

Benefits:
- Faster startup time
- Memory saved if patterns not used
- Hot-reload ready

### 2. Bloom Filter Pre-Filter
Before running Aho-Corasick, check Bloom filter:
```rust
if !bloom_filter.possibly_contains(&text) {
    return vec![]; // Quick reject
}
// Only then run full pattern matching
```

Budget: < 1µs for Bloom check

### 3. Connection Pooling for WezTerm CLI
Keep warm connections to WezTerm mux server:
```rust
struct WezTermPool {
    connections: Vec<MuxConnection>,
    max_size: usize,
}
```

Benefits:
- Avoid process spawn overhead
- Reduce socket churn
- Enable connection reuse

Note: Only beneficial if vendored mode or mux protocol direct access.

### 4. Memory-Efficient Output Cache
Already exists as `wa-4vx.4.12` - enhance with:
- LRU eviction by memory pressure
- Rolling hash for dedup detection
- Configurable cache size limits

### 5. Background Segment Compression
Already gated by `wa-4vx.3.11` decision - if needed:
- Compress segments older than N days
- Use zstd for good ratio and speed
- Transparent decompression on read
- Background compaction thread

### 6. Incremental FTS Sync
Ensure FTS index only processes new content:
- Track last-indexed segment per pane
- Batch index updates
- Avoid full reindex on restart

## Benchmarks
All optimizations must be validated by Criterion benchmarks:
- Baseline before optimization
- Result after optimization
- Regression tests to prevent backsliding

## Testing
- Benchmark suite for each optimization
- Memory profiling under sustained load
- No correctness regressions (same outputs)

## Success Criteria
- Startup time < 100ms (no feature flags)
- Pattern quick-reject < 1µs for non-matching text
- Memory usage < 100MB for typical workload
- CPU usage < 5% when idle

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each optimization yields measurable latency or memory improvement.
- Performance budgets are enforced in CI.
- Regression thresholds and baselines are documented.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.2.10","title":"Human CLI tests: output layer snapshots + no-ANSI guarantees","description":"# Task: Human CLI tests for output layer

## Goal
Make the human CLI output layer trustworthy and regression-resistant.

Humans will pipe wa output into files, paste it into issues, and rely on it under stress. Output regressions are user-hostile.

## Scope

### Renderer-level snapshot tests (core renderers)
Renderer-level tests (no subprocess) for the **Phase 4 baseline** human CLI:
- status/panes table renderer
- events list/panel renderer (**raw/technical** event fields)
- query/search results renderer
- workflow summary renderer
- audit feed renderer

### Explicit non-scope (covered elsewhere)
To keep priorities and dependencies sane, this bead intentionally does **not** block on Phase 4 “UX excellence extras”. Those renderers/overlays must still be tested, but they are covered by their own beads:
- Natural-language event descriptions in event rendering: `wa-0go.7`
- History timeline / rollback visualization output: `wa-5em.9`
- Suggestion overlays in status/events/errors: `wa-tp4.8`

This separation keeps the **core CLI output layer** stable early, while allowing optional overlays to evolve without blocking core ship readiness.

## Format selection behavior
- auto mode chooses rich only when explicitly told "tty" (injectable in tests)
- plain mode is stable and contains no ANSI escape sequences
- json mode is stable and uses a documented schema (may share the robot envelope)

## Key requirements
- Snapshot tests for plain output (golden text). The exact formatting should be intentional.
- Snapshot tests for json output (stable fields, stable error codes).
- Redaction expectations:
  - never print secrets in plain/rich output
  - when showing inputs, use a redacted summary consistent with audit rules

## Implementation notes
- Keep renderers pure: typed input -> String.
- Avoid dependence on real TTY detection in tests. Inject a bool or OutputMode instead.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A deliberate formatting change requires updating snapshots.
- Piped output contains no ANSI sequences by default.
- Errors follow the same structure across commands (message + hint + code).

## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add an explicit assertion that plain-mode output contains no `\x1b[` ANSI escapes.
  - Add a fake secret string in inputs and assert it never appears in renderer output.

- Snapshot discipline:
  - Snapshots should be per-renderer and small enough to review; avoid one mega-snapshot that is hard to update.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.2.11","title":"Human CLI tests: command contract tests (core commands)","description":"# Task: Human CLI command contract tests (core commands)

## Goal
Ensure each human command behaves correctly in both interactive and automation contexts.

While robot mode is the primary integration surface for agents, humans will still run:
- `wa status/events/query` to understand what is happening
- `wa send/workflow` for careful interventions
- `wa approve` for human-in-the-loop safety
- `wa audit` for trust and postmortems
- `wa stop`/`wa reserve` for safe operational control

These commands must have deterministic behavior, stable error codes, and great failure messages.

## Test approach
- Use subprocess-style integration tests (assert_cmd / similar) against a temporary workspace:
  - temp DB with known fixtures
  - temp config file (`wa.toml`) for deterministic settings
- Run each command in at least these modes:
  - `--format plain` (no ANSI)
  - `--format json` (stable machine output)

## Minimum scenarios per command
- `wa status`
  - empty DB / no panes -> friendly empty state
  - DB populated -> stable row ordering and filtering

- `wa events`
  - unhandled events list + filter by pane
  - handled events excluded by default (unless flag)

- `wa query`
  - FTS hit with snippet
  - no hits -> helpful hint

- `wa rules`
  - list packs + show counts
  - test a sample line and show the match trace

- `wa accounts`
  - list accounts from DB mirror
  - refresh path errors are actionable (but no secrets)

- `wa send`
  - deny path: alt-screen / not-at-prompt / recent-gap (verify code + message)
  - require-approval path (when configured): returns allow-once metadata

- `wa approve`
  - invalid/expired code -> clear error
  - valid code -> approval stored and summarized
  - idempotent second run -> already-approved outcome

- `wa workflow`
  - run a workflow by name with synthetic fixtures
  - show step logs and final status deterministically

- `wa audit`
  - show recent actions
  - filters and redaction behavior

- `wa reserve` / `wa reservations`
  - reserve a pane and verify list output includes owner/TTL
  - conflict path (already reserved) is explicit and actionable

- `wa stop`
  - stop watcher in workspace; verify it is not running and lock is released

## Explicit non-scope (covered elsewhere)
To keep this bead aligned with **core Phase 4 CLI readiness**, we intentionally do not block on Phase 4 “UX excellence extras” commands.

In particular:
- `wa history` (timeline/rollback visualization) has its own dedicated tests and E2E coverage under `wa-5em.9`.

## Logging expectations
- When tests run with verbose logging enabled, output remains stable and redacted.
- Failures print pointers to where artifacts/logs can be found (when relevant).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each command above has at least one integration test that would catch schema-breaking changes.
- Exit codes and error codes are stable across releases.

## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - For every command tested in plain mode, assert there are no ANSI escapes.
  - Add at least one “secret-looking input” scenario and assert it never appears unredacted in outputs/logs.

- Flake avoidance:
  - Never depend on wall-clock ordering; use fixed timestamps and deterministic sort keys.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.2.12","title":"[Human command] wa approve (grant allow-once approval code)","description":"# Task: Human command wa approve

## Goal
Provide the human-side companion to PolicyEngine RequireApproval.

When wa robot send / MCP returns RequireApproval, it should include a short allow-once code.
A human can then run:
- `wa approve <code>`

This creates a scoped, expiring approval so the originally-requested action can proceed.

## UX requirements
- Must display a clear summary of what is being approved:
  - action kind
  - pane_id / workflow_id (as applicable)
  - TTL/expiration
  - redacted input summary
- Safety confirmations:
  - if running in a TTY: prompt for confirmation unless --yes is passed
  - if not a TTY: require --yes (fail otherwise)

## Scope/guarantees
- Approvals are always:
  - scoped to a single workspace
  - scoped to a single action fingerprint
  - expiring
- wa approve must refuse unknown/expired codes.

## Integration
- Writes approval into the DB via the approval token system (`wa-4vx.8.9`).
- Records an audit entry for the approval grant.

## Testing
- Command contract tests: `wa-nu4.3.2.11`.
- E2E: `wa-4vx.10.16`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Given an allow-once code from a RequireApproval error, wa approve stores the approval and prints a success summary.
- Running wa approve twice on the same code is idempotent or yields a clear "already approved" result.

-{"id":"wa-nu4.3.2.13","title":"[Human command] wa stop (stop watcher in workspace safely)","description":"# Task: Human command wa stop

## Goal
Give users a safe, ergonomic way to stop a running `wa watch` for a given workspace.

Many users will run `wa watch` in the background (or via terminal multiplexers). Telling them to manually find and kill a PID is user-hostile.

## Behavior (v0)
- `wa stop`
  - resolves the workspace (flag/env/default)
  - reads the watch lock metadata (pid/started_at/host/version)
  - sends SIGTERM to the watcher pid (preferred) and waits for the lock to be released
  - prints a clear summary:
    - which workspace
    - which pid
    - whether shutdown was graceful

- `wa stop --force`
  - if graceful shutdown times out, escalates to SIGKILL (only with explicit `--force`)

## Safety
- Never kills an arbitrary PID:
  - only act if the lock metadata matches the workspace lock
  - require the process to be owned by the same user (best-effort check)

## UX
- TTY: rich panel output.
- Non-TTY: stable JSON.

## Testing
- Integration test:
  - start a watcher-like process that holds the lock
  - run `wa stop`
  - assert lock is released and process exits

- E2E:
  - `wa-4vx.10.21` validates stop+restart behavior and captures artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa stop` reliably stops a watcher started in the same workspace.
- `wa stop` errors are actionable when no watcher is running.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.2.14","title":"[Human command] wa undo (undo supported actions with confirmation)","description":"# Task: [Human command] `wa undo` (undo supported actions with confirmation)

## Goal
Provide a safe, human‑friendly CLI for undoing **supported** action types, using the undo engine from wa‑5em.

## Command Surface
- `wa undo --list` (list undoable actions)
- `wa undo <action-id>` (undo specific action)
- `wa undo --all-in-workflow <workflow_id>` (bulk undo)
- `--yes` to skip confirmation
- `--format json|plain` for automation

## Safety
- Show explicit summary before undo:
  - action type, pane/workflow, time, undoability reason
- Require confirmation unless `--yes`.
- If not undoable, return structured error with manual guidance.

## Output
- TTY: rich panel with action summary and result
- Non‑TTY: structured JSON with stable codes

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests:
  - list output includes undoable actions only
  - undo success path updates action log
  - undo not‑applicable returns clear error
- E2E:
  - extend rollback visualization E2E (`wa-5em.9`) to call `wa undo` and verify state + logs

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa undo` is safe by default (confirmation required).
- Structured output includes action ids + undo results.
- Errors include remediation hints when undo is not applicable.

## Goal
Prevent accidental exposure when running aggregator/agent networking.

Distributed mode turns wa into a networked system; the default posture must be “safe and local”, with explicit opt-in for anything remotely reachable.

## Requirements
### Bind defaults
- Aggregator binds to localhost by default.
- Public bind requires an explicit, scary flag (e.g., `--dangerous-bind-any`) and prints a prominent warning.

### Authentication
- Simple shared auth token between agent and aggregator (v0):
  - sent in a header or the first message
  - constant-time compare
  - never logged

### Replay / injection resistance
- Basic replay protection:
  - monotonic sequence numbers per agent session
  - reject stale/duplicate sequences

- Rate limiting / connection limits:
  - cap concurrent connections
  - cap messages/sec per connection

### Redaction
- Aggregator logs and any exposed query surfaces must use wa’s redaction rules.

## Testing strategy
- Unit tests:
  - token verification rejects wrong/missing token
  - replay checks reject duplicate/stale sequence numbers

- Integration tests:
  - unauthenticated client cannot inject fake events/segments
  - authenticated client can stream
  - logs do not contain token values

## Acceptance Criteria
- An unauthenticated client cannot inject fake events/segments.
- Public bind is never the default.
- Replay/injection controls are present and test-covered.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.3.5","title":"Distributed E2E tests: agent→aggregator stream, persistence, CLI visibility","description":"# Task: Distributed E2E tests (feature distributed)

## Goal
Validate distributed mode end-to-end so it can be trusted when enabled:
- agent → aggregator streaming
- persistence + indexing
- CLI/robot visibility of aggregated data

Distributed mode is optional, but if we ship it, it must not be flaky.

## E2E harness requirements
- Must run **offline** with no real WezTerm required.
- Prefer a deterministic “fake agent” producer that emits:
  - segments (PaneDelta)
  - explicit GAP events
  - detections/events
  - out-of-order or duplicated messages (to test robustness)

- Spin up an aggregator instance bound to localhost with an ephemeral port.
- Capture artifacts:
  - aggregator logs
  - agent logs
  - a small exported DB snapshot

## Test runner integration
- Must run via the shared E2E runner (`./scripts/e2e_test.sh`) and follow the harness artifacts contract.
- The runner should allow skipping this case when `--features distributed` is not enabled.

## Required scenarios
1) Happy path
   - agent streams a few segments
   - aggregator persists them
   - CLI/robot query surfaces can retrieve them

2) GAP preservation
   - agent emits a gap
   - aggregator records it
   - downstream queries reflect the discontinuity

3) Robustness
   - duplicated messages do not create duplicate persisted rows (or are deduped deterministically)
   - out-of-order messages either:
     - are re-ordered safely, OR
     - are recorded as a gap + diagnostic (explicitly choose and test)

4) Security baseline
   - auth token required when configured
   - logs + responses remain redacted

## Observability expectations
- Structured logs with correlation ids:
  - stream connection/session id
  - pane id
  - message counts
- On failure, E2E script prints where artifacts were written.

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`).
- Mark it as **non-default** unless `--features distributed` (or equivalent) is enabled.

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- An E2E script (or `cargo test --features distributed`) can spin up fake agent + aggregator and assert:
  - segments arrive
  - gaps are preserved
  - detections/events arrive
  - aggregated data is visible via CLI/robot query
- Failures produce actionable logs + artifacts.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a negative security scenario: missing/invalid auth token must be rejected and logged (redacted) with stable error codes.
  - Assert required artifacts exist (agent logs, aggregator logs, DB snapshot/export) before declaring PASS.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.3.6","title":"Distributed mode readiness criteria + rollout checklist","description":"# Task: Distributed mode readiness criteria + rollout checklist

## Goal
Define when distributed mode is safe to enable and how to roll it out without surprises.

## Why
Distributed mode introduces network and security risks. We need explicit criteria and a checklist before enabling it by default.

## Checklist topics
- Security baseline met (auth token, bind defaults, replay protection).
- Observability present (logs, metrics, health snapshots).
- E2E coverage exists for agent→aggregator flows.
- Backward compatibility for wire protocol versions.
- Failure modes documented (agent disconnects, partial data, gaps).

## Deliverables
- A go/no-go checklist stored in this bead.
- Decision on default feature gating (off by default, explicit `--features distributed`).
- Documentation notes for users.

## Testing
- Validate that the checklist references existing tests/E2E cases by ID.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Distributed mode is blocked until all checklist items are met.
- Rollout steps are documented and self-contained.

-{"id":"wa-nu4.4.3.7","title":"Distributed mode scope decision (when multi-machine coordination is needed)","description":"# Task: Distributed mode scope decision (when multi-machine coordination is needed)

## Goal
Resolve PLAN.md Open Question #6: **when do we actually need cross-machine wa instances to communicate?**

This is a decision gate: we should not invest in distributed mode unless there is a clear, measured user benefit.

## Why this matters (user value)
Distributed mode adds:
- operational burden (deploying and updating `wa-agent`)
- security surface area (network listeners, auth tokens)
- additional failure modes

If users do not need it yet, we should defer and keep the core system simpler and more reliable.

## Inputs to evaluate
1. **Scale triggers**
   - Number of remote domains/panes where CLI polling becomes slow or flaky.
   - Average tail latency and gap rates with current CLI/Lua approaches.

2. **User-story triggers**
   - Does any priority user story *require* cross-machine aggregation (e.g., H1 across many hosts)?
   - Are there cases where local-only wa cannot observe all panes reliably?

3. **Reliability/operations**
   - Does a single workstation failure represent an unacceptable single point of failure?
   - Do we need to keep local capture alive when aggregator is offline?

4. **Security/constraints**
   - Are there environments where outbound agent→aggregator streaming is forbidden?
   - Are there compliance constraints that require local-only capture?

## Work items
- Collect baseline metrics from the CLI/Lua-only mode:
  - capture latency
  - gap rate
  - CPU usage on busy mux servers
- Define **thresholds** that justify distributed mode, e.g.:
  - N domains/panes beyond which latency or gap rate exceeds target.
  - Latency SLO violation (e.g., > 250ms p95) under normal load.
- Decide **go/no-go** for distributed mode in the next release cycle and document:
  - timeline (v0.2? later?)
  - minimal MVP scope if “go”
  - deferral rationale if “no-go”

## Deliverables
- Decision note recorded in this bead with measurable thresholds and timeline.
- If “go”:
  - update distributed epic to reflect MVP scope and prerequisites.
- If “no-go”:
  - document the specific triggers that would cause a re-evaluation.

## Testing / validation
- Use existing perf/metrics harnesses (e.g., `wa-4vx.10.2`, `wa-nu4.3.4.2`) to gather baseline numbers.
- Evidence should be reproducible (logs or summary table in the decision note).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Decision is documented with thresholds tied to measurable data.
- The decision explicitly references user-story impact (what it enables or why it’s unnecessary).
- Re-evaluation triggers are clear and actionable.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.4","title":"[EPIC] Multi-agent coordination: swarm workflows + skills/docs for agents","description":"# [EPIC] Multi-agent coordination

## Mission
Turn wa from a single-pane automator into a coordination substrate for many agent panes.

## What this includes
- "swarm" workflows that coordinate multiple panes safely
- guidance/docs/skills so agents can use wa effectively
- optional integrations that help unstick agents (e.g., ast-grep scans)

## Principles
- Coordination is still built from the same primitives:
  - observe
  - decide
  - act (guarded)
  - verify

## Definition of done
- At least one multi-pane workflow exists and is safe-by-default.
- Docs/skills explain how to use wa for swarm operations.


## Success Criteria
- Coordination primitives enable multi-pane workflows safely (locks, scoped broadcasts, verification) without spamming terminals.
- Skills/docs make it easy for agents to use coordination features correctly.
- Tests cover group lock semantics and safe broadcast behavior.


## Testing
- Unit/integration tests:
  - Group lock semantics (no deadlocks, deterministic ordering).
  - Safe broadcast behavior: verify-before-act per pane and rate limiting to avoid spam.

- E2E (optional but valuable):
  - A multi-pane scenario that coordinates two dummy panes and produces artifacts proving no duplicate sends.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Definition of Done.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.4.1","title":"Coordination primitives: multi-pane selection, group locks, safe broadcast semantics","description":"# Task: Coordination primitives

## Goal
Define the primitives required for safe multi-pane workflows ("swarm" behavior) without turning wa into a chaos machine.

Multi-pane automation is powerful but risky: we need deterministic selection, locking, and per-pane auditing.

## Concepts
### Pane groups
- Define grouping strategies:
  - by domain
  - by inferred agent
  - by project (cwd-based)
- Group selection must be deterministic and explainable.

### Group locks
- Build on per-pane workflow locks to support multi-pane coordination:
  - lock acquisition across N panes is all-or-nothing (or explicitly partial with clear reporting)
  - timeouts and deadlock avoidance

### Safe broadcast semantics
- Observe each pane first.
- Only act on panes that pass preconditions (prompt state, no gap, not alt-screen, etc.).
- Execute actions with policy gating + rate limiting.
- Record per-pane results:
  - allowed/denied
  - verification success/failure
  - timing

## Testing strategy
- Unit tests with synthetic panes:
  - group selection and determinism
  - lock acquisition behavior
  - partial failure reporting

## Acceptance Criteria
- A workflow can target N panes and produce an auditable per-pane outcome.
- Preconditions prevent “spray and pray” broadcasting.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.4.4.2","title":"Implement multi-pane workflow(s): coordinate_agents (safe broadcast + verify)","description":"# Task: Implement multi-pane workflow(s)

## Goal
Add at least one real coordination workflow that demonstrates swarm value.

## Candidate workflows
1. `coordinate_agents.reread_context`
   - target: all panes matching agent types
   - action: send a context refresh prompt (agent-specific)
   - verify: wait for prompt boundary/idle

2. `coordinate_agents.pause_all`
   - target: all panes
   - action: send safe stop/pause keystrokes (policy-gated)

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests with synthetic panes:
  - precondition filtering prevents "spray and pray"
  - per-pane outcomes are recorded deterministically
  - policy denials are handled per-pane without aborting the whole run (unless explicitly configured)

- E2E tests (optional, but preferred if we ship this):
  - run against a small set of dummy panes that echo input
  - verify only eligible panes receive messages
  - capture artifacts (audit + workflow logs)
